attachment.content:
- make: Run the project Makefile (regenerates Xcode project via xcodegen and opens it).
- pr: Wrap current uncommitted changes into a Feature/RAD-XXXX branch and open a Bitbucket PR.
- post-merge: Sync main after a PR has been merged (git checkout main + pull --ff-only + make).
- pdf-viewer:open: Open a PDF in the interactive viewer
- pdf-viewer:fill-form: Fill PDF form fields interactively with live visual feedback
- pdf-viewer:annotate: Collaboratively annotate a PDF — propose markup, review together, iterate
- pdf-viewer:sign: Place a signature or initials image on a PDF
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:extract-design: Extract the full design language from any website URL. Produces 8 output files including AI-optimized markdown, visual HTML preview, Tailwind config, React theme, shadcn/ui theme, Figma variables, W3C design tokens, and CSS variables. Also runs WCAG accessibility scoring. Use when user says 'extract design', 'get design system', 'design language', 'design tokens', 'what colors/fonts does this site use', or '/extract-design'.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork
- pdf-viewer:view-pdf
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review
- security-review

content.text:
# Organizations

## **radiacode**

**Web URL:** https://radiacode.sentry.io
**Region URL:** https://us.sentry.io

# Using this information

- The organization's name is the identifier for the organization, and is used in many tools for `organizationSlug`.
- If a tool supports passing in the `regionUrl`, you MUST pass in the correct value shown above for each organization.
- For Sentry's Cloud Service (sentry.io), always use the regionUrl to ensure requests go to the correct region.

content.thinking:
Now I have the details. Let me look at the issue in context.

The crash is:
- **EXC_BAD_ACCESS** (fatal, unhandled)
- `KERN_INVALID_ADDRESS at 0x2` - classic nil/dangling pointer issue (0x2 is very close to 0x0, likely an object with offset)
- **Culprit**: `LocationManager.updateCurrentLocation` at `LocationManager.swift:75`
- The line is: `locationManager?.startUpdatingLocation()`

The stacktrace shows:


The crash is happening during deallocation of objects within CoreLocation internals. The `KERN_INVALID_ADDRESS at 0x2` and the release chain suggest this is likely a race condition where `locationManager` is being deallocated while CoreLocation is still using it, or the object is being accessed after it's already been freed.

Looking at the code flow, `locationManager` is being accessed from within a checked continuation when `currentLocation` is called, which then triggers `updateCurrentLocation` and `startUpdatingLocation()`. The issue is probably that `locationManager` is being accessed from a concurrent Swift context while simultaneously being deallocated, causing the Objective-C deallocation chain to crash when trying to release objects that are no longer valid.

content.thinking:
Now I understand the full context. Let me analyze the crash:

**Crash**: `EXC_BAD_ACCESS: KERN_INVALID_ADDRESS at 0x2` in `LocationManager.updateCurrentLocation` at line 75:
```swift
locationManager?.startUpdatingLocation()
```

The stacktrace shows the crash happens in the dealloc chain of Objective-C objects inside CoreLocation internals, specifically during `objc_release`.

**Root cause analysis:**

The key issue here is **thread safety** / **race condition**:

1. `CLLocationManager` must be created and used on the main thread


2. The `locationManager` gets initialized asynchronously in a `Task` marked with `@MainActor` inside `init()`, but `updateCurrentLocation()` can be called from multiple contexts—some through the main thread via the authorization status publisher, and others through `currentLocation()` which runs on arbitrary threads via `withCheckedThrowingContinuation`. Since `currentLocation()` is an `async` function without explicit main thread enforcement, it can invoke `updateCurrentLocation()` from a background thread, causing the crash when trying to access the location manager.

3. The `locationContinuations` array is being mutated from multiple threads without any synchronization, which compounds the thread-safety issue.

4. Looking at the crash stack trace, the problem traces back to `updateCurrentLocation()` calling `locationManager?.startUpdatingLocation()` from a non-main thread, which then fails during CoreLocation's internal object deallocation—the `KERN_INVALID_ADDRESS` in the `objc_release` chain suggests an over-release or use-after-free scenario caused by the thread violation.

The solution is to mark `LocationManager` as `@MainActor` to enforce that all CoreLocation operations happen on the main thread, or at minimum mark both `currentLocation()` and `updateCurrentLocation()` as `@MainActor` to guarantee thread safety for the continuation handling. The real issue is that wrapping initialization in a Task creates a race condition—`updateCurrentLocation()` might be called before the manager is actually set up. If I mark the entire class as `@MainActor`, the `init()` method already runs on the main thread, so the Task wrapper becomes unnecessary and I can initialize `CLLocationManager` directly.

Defining the class structure...

Actually, I'm realizing the `@MainActor` annotation helps but doesn't fully solve the problem—the real issue is that peculiar crash address `0x2`, which suggests something's being dereferenced incorrectly rather than a threading violation.

Looking at the deallocation stack trace, this appears to be memory corruption happening during cleanup of arrays and dictionaries, likely from a race condition where two threads are simultaneously modifying retain counts, or CoreLocation itself isn't handling concurrent access safely.

The real issue is that `updateCurrentLocation()` is being called from a background thread, but CoreLocation's internal machinery—including its NSDictionary and NSArray objects—expects to run on the main thread. The fix is to mark the entire `LocationManager` class with `@MainActor` to guarantee all method calls happen on the main thread, which eliminates the need for the `Task { @MainActor in ... }` wrapper in the initializer.

Setting up the authorization status observer...

The issue is that `updateCurrentLocation()` needs to be marked as `@MainActor` since it's called from both the main thread sink and from `currentLocation()` which might be called from any context—without that annotation, Swift won't properly enforce thread safety. The fix is to make the entire `LocationManager` class `@MainActor` so all its methods are guaranteed to run on the main thread. The cleanest approach is to mark the entire `LocationManager` class as `@MainActor` since `CLLocationManager` requires main thread access anyway, which ensures all property access and method calls are automatically serialized on the main actor without needing explicit annotations on individual methods. ), this could cause issues

Actually SwiftUI Views are `@MainActor`, so this should be fine.

Let me formulate the fix:

The simplest, most correct fix is to add `@MainActor` to the class. This ensures:
1. All method calls on `LocationManager` are dispatched to the main thread
2. All CLLocationManager operations happen on the main thread (required by CoreLocation)
3. The `locationContinuations` array is protected from concurrent access
4. The crash in `startUpdatingLocation()` from a background thread is prevented

But wait, should I also remove the `Task { @MainActor in }` wrapper? If `LocationManager` is `@MainActor`, then `init()` runs on the main actor already, so we could initialize `CLLocationManager` directly without wrapping it in a Task. The original code probably uses the Task wrapper to defer initialization until after `init()` completes, which creates a window where the Combine sink might fire before `locationManager` is set—but the optional chaining handles that gracefully. Making the class `@MainActor` and removing the Task would be cleaner, though it's a larger refactor than just adding the annotation.

For now, I'll propose the minimal fix: add `@MainActor` to the class definition. I should present this analysis clearly and wait for confirmation before making changes.

content.thinking:
The user says they want to work in the background - meaning they want CLLocationManager to work on a background thread/actor, not on the main thread. Let me re-read the code and think about this differently.

Actually, the user might be saying they want the location operations to NOT require being on the main thread. But CLLocationManager itself HAS to be created and used on the main thread (or at least on the thread/run loop where it was created). This is a CoreLocation requirement.

Wait, maybe the user is confused about what `@MainActor` means in this context. Let me think...

Actually, maybe the user wants location updates to work in the background (as in background location updates), and they're already using:


```swift
locationManager.allowsBackgroundLocationUpdates = true
locationManager.showsBackgroundLocationIndicator = true
```

That's for background location tracking (the app receives location updates when suspended/backgrounded). That's different from which thread CLLocationManager runs on. Or maybe the user is saying that `currentLocation()` being called from a background context is intentional - they want async work to happen off the main thread. But the issue is that CLLocationManager REQUIRES main thread. So the fix needs to ensure CLLocationManager is used from the main thread while still allowing callers to await from background contexts. The solution would be to mark only the CLLocationManager-specific operations as requiring the main thread.

The cleanest approach with Swift concurrency is to mark the methods that directly interact with CLLocationManager as `@MainActor`, then have callers use `Task { @MainActor in }` to dispatch the work to the main thread while still allowing the overall function to be awaitable from any context.

Actually, I'm realizing the better solution is to make the entire `LocationManager` class `@MainActor` since CLLocationManager requires main thread access anyway. When `currentLocation()` is marked as `@MainActor async`, it's still callable from background tasks—the caller just awaits it and Swift concurrency automatically handles hopping to the main actor, executing the work, and returning the result without blocking.

The user might be asking for background location updates to continue working when the app is backgrounded, which means `allowsBackgroundLocationUpdates = true` should already handle that. But if they want `currentLocation()` callable from a background task without crashes, the issue is that `updateCurrentLocation()` is calling CLLocationManager methods off the main thread. The fix is to ensure those CLLocationManager calls specifically dispatch to the main thread.

Writing code examples... Writing the async API...

Actually, I'm realizing the core issue: I need to keep all `CLLocationManager` interactions on the main thread while allowing the public `currentLocation()` function to be called from any context. The solution is marking `updateCurrentLocation()` as `@MainActor` so it safely manages the location manager, while the async wrapper handles the thread bridging through Swift concurrency.

The user might be confused about what `@MainActor` means for background work—it doesn't block the main thread when called from a background task, it just ensures the code runs on the main actor's context, which is necessary for CoreLocation's requirements. The real fix is to wrap the CLLocationManager calls in a main thread dispatch without marking the entire function as `@MainActor` — this way the async function stays callable from background contexts, but the location manager operations execute where they need to be. This preserves the API's flexibility while respecting Apple's threading requirements.

However, I also need to check for other potential data races, particularly with `trackingEnabled` which gets accessed from `updateCurrentLocation()` and modified in `startTracking()`/`stopTracking()` — if those are called from background tasks, there could be race conditions there too.

The real fix is to ensure the continuation gets appended on the main thread by wrapping that logic in a main dispatch call, which also handles the `updateCurrentLocation()` invocation safely.

Option C with `@MainActor` is cleaner architecturally, but I should clarify to the user that marking the function this way doesn't block background callers—it just automatically hops to the main thread, executes, and returns to the caller's context. The question is whether they want a fully background-compatible approach or if main-thread safety is acceptable for their use case.

I'll propose a minimal fix using `DispatchQueue.main.async` inside `updateCurrentLocation()` to handle the `CLLocationManager` requirement while keeping the public API callable from any context.

content.text:
Понял. Тут важно разделить два разных понятия «фон»:

1. **Фоновый режим iOS** (`allowsBackgroundLocationUpdates = true`) — уже стоит, это правильно.
2. **Фоновый Swift Task / не-MainActor** — вот здесь проблема: `CLLocationManager` требует главного потока по техническим ограничениям Apple, и это не обойти.

Краш возникает именно потому, что `startUpdatingLocation()` вызывается с фонового потока. Чтобы сохранить возможность вызывать `currentLocation()` из любого контекста, но при этом не трогать весь класс — минимальный точечный фикс:

```swift
func currentLocation() async throws -> Coordinates? {
    try await withCheckedThrowingContinuation { continuation in
        DispatchQueue.main.async { [weak self] in
            self?.locationContinuations.append(continuation)
            self?.updateCurrentLocation()
        }
    }
}
```

Тогда `currentLocation()` вызывается откуда угодно, но `startUpdatingLocation()` и работа с `locationContinuations` — всегда на главном потоке.

Применить?

content.thinking:
The user wants to understand how to get current location data in the background. Let me look at their code and understand what they mean by "background":

1. They want to call `currentLocation()` from a background Swift Task / non-MainActor context
2. OR they want location updates when the app is backgrounded

Looking at their code:
```swift
func currentLocation() async throws -> Coordinates? {
    try await withCheckedThrowingContinuation { continuation in
        locationContinuations.append(continuation)


```

This is a one-shot async function that returns the current location. The issue is that it's being called from a non-main-actor context.

I think the user wants to be able to call `await locationManager.currentLocation()` from a background actor/task. The fix I proposed with `DispatchQueue.main.async` would handle this.

But actually, looking at the crash stack:
```
at withCheckedContinuation<T> (<unknown>)
at closure in LocationManager.currentLocation (LocationManager.swift:69)
        updateCurrentLocation()
```

The issue is clear - `currentLocation()` is being called from a non-main-thread, and the problem is that `withCheckedThrowingContinuation` needs to be invoked on the main thread since it's managing location updates that require main-thread access.

With the `DispatchQueue.main.async` wrapper, callers can invoke `await locationManager.currentLocation()` from any background task and it'll transparently handle the thread switching internally.

content.text:
С моим фиксом (`DispatchQueue.main.async` внутри) — всё уже работает как нужно. Вы вызываете с любого фона:

```swift
// Из любого Task / фонового актора — без изменений
let coords = try await locationManager.currentLocation()
```

`DispatchQueue.main.async` внутри `currentLocation()` делает внутренний прыжок на главный поток прозрачно для вызывающего кода: CLLocationManager стартует на main, колбэк делегата возвращает результат через continuation, и управление возвращается обратно в ваш фоновый Task.

Схема:
```
Background Task
  └─ await currentLocation()
       └─ DispatchQueue.main.async {
            locationContinuations.append(...)
            startUpdatingLocation()   ← main thread ✓
          }
            └─ didUpdateLocations (main thread)
                 └─ continuation.resume(...)
  ← возврат в Background Task с результатом
```

Применить фикс?

Env vars (`JIRA_*`, `BITBUCKET_*`) are loaded via `.claude/settings.local.json` — do NOT `source ~/.zshrc`.

Summary of the flow:

2. Show user the summary + `git diff HEAD --stat`. Ask: "коммитим как `RAD-1183 <summary>`?" — **this is the single gate**, do not ask again after.
3. After "да":
   - `git checkout -b Feature/RAD-1183`.
   - Build verify: `xcodebuild -project RadiaCode.xcodeproj -scheme Radiacode -destination 'platform=iOS Simulator,name=iPhone Air' -configuration Debug build CODE_SIGNING_ALLOWED=NO`. If `RadiaCode.xcodeproj` missing, run `xcodegen generate` first.
   - `git add -u` (stages all tracked modifications + deletions) and `git add` any new files explicitly. Do NOT use `git add -A` (avoid `.claude/` etc.).
   - Commit with message `RAD-1183 <summary verbatim from Jira>` plus the `Co-Authored-By` trailer.
   - Push: `git -c credential.helper='!f() { echo "username=mvkozin"; echo "password=$BITBUCKET_API_TOKEN"; }; f' push -u origin Feature/RAD-1183`.
   - Create PR: `curl -sS -u "$BITBUCKET_EMAIL:$BITBUCKET_API_TOKEN" -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" "https://api.bitbucket.org/2.0/repositories/radiacode/radiacode-ios/pullrequests" -d '{"title": "RAD-1183 <summary>", "source": {"branch": {"name": "Feature/RAD-1183"}}, "destination": {"branch": {"name": "main"}}, "close_source_branch": true}'`.
4. Report PR URL. Do not assign reviewers — user does that manually.

- Commit/PR title: `RAD-XXXX <summary>` — summary verbatim from Jira, no paraphrase or translation.
- Base branch: `main`.
- Bitbucket REST API uses email; git over HTTPS uses username `mvkozin`.
